title: Five Aggregates
text: # Five Aggregates ## Concept The five aggregates provide a framework for analysing what is taken as a self. In this corpus they are often treated in relation to emptiness, instability, and deconstruction of identification. ## The five 1. Form 2. Feeling 3. Perception 4. Preparations / formations 5. Consciousness ## Best sermon entry points - Sermon 6 - Sermon 24 ## Start here - [[consciousness-vinnana]] - [[sankhara]]

title: Cessation
text: # Cessation ## Concept Cessation is a core key to understanding Nibbāna in this corpus. The sermons often explain liberation through cessation of conditions rather than through positing an eternal essence. ## Key lines of discussion - cessation of craving - cessation within dependent origination - cessation of name-and-form and consciousness in reciprocal dependence - cessation as stilling and peace ## Best sermon entry points - Sermon 1 - Sermon 3 - Sermon 15 ## Start here - [[nibbana]] - [[dependent-origination]] - [[name-and-form]]

title: Consciousness (Viññāṇa)
text: # Consciousness (Viññāṇa) ## Concept Consciousness is treated in this corpus as dependently arisen rather than as a permanent essence. Major discussions connect consciousness with name-and-form, the six sense-based consciousnesses, and the cessation dynamic relevant to Nibbāna. ## Key lines of discussion - Consciousness and name-and-form mutually condition one another. - There are six consciousnesses tied to the six sense spheres. - Consciousness is not a substantial self. - Some sermons discuss *anidassana viññāṇa* (non-manifestative consciousness). ## Where to start - [[sermon-03-summary]] - [[sermon-14-summary]] - [[sermon-24-summary]] ## Best sermon entry points - Sermon 3: consciousness and name-and-form - Sermon 14: non-manifestative consciousness and dwelling - Sermon 24: six consciousnesses and dependent arising

title: Craving and Clinging
text: # Craving and Clinging ## Concept Craving and clinging are central to bondage and continuity in saṃsāra. The sermons repeatedly connect Nibbāna with the destruction of craving and with disentanglement from grasping. ## Key lines of discussion - craving as fuel for becoming - clinging as appropriation and holding on - release through dispassion and relinquishment ## Best sermon entry points - Sermon 1 - Sermon 15 - Sermon 23 ## Start here - [[nibbana]] - [[dependent-origination]]

title: Dependent Origination
text: # Dependent Origination ## Concept A central theme of the corpus. Dependent origination is treated not as abstract metaphysics alone, but as a practical key to understanding arising, cessation, bondage, and release. ## Key lines of discussion - conditional arising and conditional cessation - the link structure beginning with ignorance and formations - consciousness and name-and-form as a crucial reciprocal relation - insight into dependent origination as a path to disentanglement ## Best sermon entry points - Sermon 2 - Sermon 3 - Sermon 24 ## Start here - [[sermon-03-summary]] - [[sermon-24-summary]]

title: Five Hindrances
text: # Five Hindrances ## Concept The five hindrances are mental obstacles that obstruct clarity, concentration, and liberating insight. In this corpus, the clearest practical discussion is linked to *dhammānupassanā* in the *Satipaṭṭhāna* framework and to the work of right endeavour. ## The five 1. Sensual desire 2. Ill will 3. Sloth and torpor 4. Restlessness and worry 5. Doubt ## Where to start - [[sermon-31-summary]] - [[sermon-15-summary]] - [[Nibbana - The Mind Stilled/36 Sermon 31|Sermon 31]] - [[Nibbana - The Mind Stilled/20 Sermon 15|Sermon 15]] ## Important notes - Sermon 31 discusses the hindrances under *dhammānupassanā* and connects them to right effort. - Sermon 15 links discursive thought with the abandoning of mental hindrances. - Not every sermon lists all five individually, so concept questions should consult both this topic file and the cited sermons. ## Citation guide Prefer citing Sermon 31 when the question is about practical observation and abandonment of hindrances.

title: Master Index
text: # Master Index This folder contains editorial guides to the sermon corpus. ## Topic maps - [[five-hindrances]] - [[consciousness-vinnana]] - [[dependent-origination]] - [[name-and-form]] - [[nibbana]] - [[sankhara]] - [[craving-and-clinging]] - [[aggregates]] - [[satipatthana]] - [[cessation]] ## Glossary - [[nivarana]] - [[vinnana]] - [[namarupa]] - [[sankhara]] - [[tanha]] - [[upadana]] - [[nirodha]] ## Sermon summaries - [[sermon-03-summary]] - [[sermon-14-summary]] - [[sermon-24-summary]] - [[sermon-31-summary]] Use these files as navigational aids. Canonical quotations remain in the sermon files.

title: Name-and-Form
text: # Name-and-Form ## Concept Name-and-form is one of the most important structural themes in the corpus. It is closely linked with consciousness and helps explain how lived experience and saṃsāric continuity are configured. ## Key lines of discussion - reciprocal relation between consciousness and name-and-form - cessation of one linked to cessation of the other - practical significance for understanding the experiential world ## Best sermon entry points - Sermon 3 - Sermon 24 ## Start here - [[sermon-03-summary]] - [[consciousness-vinnana]] - [[namarupa]]

title: Nibbāna
text: # Nibbāna ## Concept The whole sermon series is oriented around clarifying the meaning of Nibbāna from the early discourses. It is approached as cessation, relinquishment, dispassion, and the stilling of all preparations. ## Key lines of discussion - destruction of craving - cessation and dispassion - stilling of all preparations - relinquishment of acquisitions and supports ## Best sermon entry points - Sermon 1 - Introduction - Sermon 14 - Sermon 15 ## Start here - Sermon 1 - Introduction - [[consciousness-vinnana]]

title: Saṅkhāra
text: # Saṅkhāra ## Concept Saṅkhāra is a major interpretive term in the corpus. Depending on context it bears the sense of preparations, formations, activities, or fabrications relevant to dependent arising and the stilling that culminates in Nibbāna. ## Key lines of discussion - saṅkhāra in dependent origination - the stilling of all preparations - relation to intentional activity and constructing tendencies ## Best sermon entry points - Sermon 1 - Sermon 2 - Sermon 3 ## Start here - [[dependent-origination]] - [[sermon-03-summary]]

title: Satipaṭṭhāna
text: # Satipaṭṭhāna ## Concept The sermons use the *Satipaṭṭhāna* framework as a practical doorway to insight. In later sermons this becomes especially important for themes like hindrances, mental observation, and right effort. ## Key lines of discussion - mindfulness as clear knowing of arising and passing - *dhammānupassanā* as an analytical contemplative framework - hindrances as objects of understanding and abandonment ## Best sermon entry points - Sermon 31 - Sermon 32 ## Start here - [[five-hindrances]] - [[sermon-31-summary]]

title: Nāmarūpa
text: # Nāmarūpa Usually translated as name-and-form. In this corpus it is especially important in its reciprocal relation with consciousness. ## Main references - Sermon 3 - Sermon 24

title: Nirodha
text: # Nirodha Cessation. One of the central interpretive keys of the series and essential for understanding Nibbāna. ## Main references - Sermon 1 - Sermon 3 - Sermon 15

title: Nīvaraṇa
text: # Nīvaraṇa Pāli term for the hindrances: mental obstacles that block collectedness and insight. ## Standard five - sensual desire - ill will - sloth and torpor - restlessness and worry - doubt ## Main references in this corpus - Sermon 31 - Sermon 15

title: Saṅkhāra
text: # Saṅkhāra A key Pāli term discussed in the sermons in relation to dependent origination and the stilling of all preparations. ## Main references - Sermon 1 - Sermon 2 - Sermon 3

title: Taṇhā
text: # Taṇhā Craving. A central driver of bondage and becoming, repeatedly contrasted with the dispassion and cessation associated with Nibbāna. ## Main references - Sermon 1 - Sermon 15 - Sermon 23

title: Upādāna
text: # Upādāna Clinging or grasping. Closely linked with craving and becoming in dependent origination. ## Main references - Sermon 15 - Sermon 23

title: Viññāṇa
text: # Viññāṇa Usually translated as consciousness. In this corpus it is discussed in relation to dependent arising, name-and-form, the sixfold division of consciousness, and cessation. ## Main references - Sermon 3 - Sermon 14 - Sermon 24

title: Sermon 3 Summary
text: # Sermon 3 Summary ## Main themes - dependent arising - the vortex between consciousness and name-and-form - cessation through breaking that reciprocal support ## Why this matters This is one of the best starting points in the corpus for questions about consciousness, name-and-form, and the structure of saṃsāric continuity.

title: Sermon 14 Summary
text: # Sermon 14 Summary ## Main themes - the *Dhātuvibhaṅgasutta* - consciousness as tending to dwell or abide - non-manifestative consciousness ## Why this matters Useful for questions about the nature of consciousness, its support, and its transformation in relation to detachment.

title: Sermon 24 Summary
text: # Sermon 24 Summary ## Main themes - the six consciousnesses - dependent arising on a dyad - consciousness as conditioned rather than substantial ## Why this matters A strong sermon for explicit discussion of consciousness and its sixfold differentiation.

title: Sermon 31 Summary
text: # Sermon 31 Summary ## Main themes - *satipaṭṭhāna* - *dhammānupassanā* - hindrances as objects of mindful comprehension - relation between hindrances and right effort ## Why this matters This is the main entry point in the corpus for practical discussion of the hindrances and their abandoning.
